<img src="topics/images/meme_comments.jpg" alt="Meme-style image where someone puts on progressively more clown makeup as they explain why they don't need to leave code comments" width="40%" align="right">

## Code Comments

:::{.callout-tip icon="false" collapse="false"}
### {{< fa brain >}} Learning Objectives

After completing this topic you will be able to: 

- <u>Explain</u> the difference between "what" and "why" comments
- <u>Create</u> useful comments in code

:::

A "comment" in a script is a human readable, non-coding line that helps give context for the code. In R (and Python), comment lines start with a hashtag (#). Including comments is a low effort way of both (A) creating internal documentation for the script and (B) increasing the reproducibility of the script. It is difficult to include "too many" comments, so when in doubt: add more comments!

There are two major strategies for comments and either or both might make sense for your project.

### "What" Comments

Comments describe _what_ the code is doing.

- Benefits: allows team members to understand workflow without code literacy
- Risks: rationale for code not explicit 

```{.r}
# Remove all pine trees from dataset
no_pine_df <- dplyr::filter(full_df, genus != "Pinus")
```

### "Why" Comments

Comments describe _rationale_ and/or _context_ for code.

- Benefits: built-in documentation for team decisions
- Risks: assumes everyone can read code

```{.r}
# Cone-bearing plants are not comparable with other plants in dataset
no_pine_df <- dplyr::filter(full_df, genus != "Pinus")
```

::::{.callout-warning icon="false"}
### {{<fa rocket >}} Activity: Comment on Comments

:::{.panel-tabset}
### Part 1 - Discuss

With a partner, discuss the following questions:

- When you write comments, do you focus more on the "what" or the "why"?
- What would you estimate is the ratio of code lines to comment lines in your code?
    - 1:1 being every code line has one comment line
- If you have revisited old code, were your comments helpful?
    - How could you make them more helpful?
- In what ways do you think you would need to change your commenting style for a team project?

### Part 2 - Revisit Old Code

Individually, pick a script from an old project (ideally one you haven't worked on recently), then:

1. Scan through it and try to identify the main purpose(s) of the code
2. Ask yourself the following questions:
    - Are there any areas where you're _not sure_ what the code is doing and/or why it is included?
    - Do you think an external expert would be able to read and understand your code?
3. Take a few minutes and add comments to clarify any vague areas you identified
    - Don't edit the code itself, just add/edit comment lines!

### Part 3 - Discuss Again

With your partner from part 1:

1. Exchange your edited code files
2. Read your partner's code and identify what you think the main purpose of the code is
    - Don't try to run it! Just see what you can glean from reading alone
3. Compare what you think the code does with what the original author intended it to do

:::
::::
